addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Glob, Grep, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- session-start-hook: Creating and developing startup hooks for Claude Code on the web. Use when the user wants to set up a repository for Claude Code on the web, create a SessionStart hook to ensure their project can run tests and linters during web sessions.
- adopt-claudinite: Bootstrap Claudinite into a consuming repo — mount, hooks, checks, skills. Use when asked to bootstrap, adopt, or set up Claudinite, or to baseline a repo to pick up updates.
- authoring-agent-docs: How to write instruction files coding agents follow reliably. Use before writing or editing any Claude instruction doc — a project CLAUDE.md, a convention doc, a routine spec.
- bug-investigation: Method for investigating a bug and pinning down its root cause. Use when investigating a bug report, when a fix didn't hold or a bug recurs, or when a report doesn't reproduce against main.
- bump-version: Raise the project's version — a minor bump by default. Use when the owner says "bump version".
- engineering-practices: General software-engineering practices — naming by scope, single-source-of-truth with drift guards, GENERATED-file discipline, earning each dependency, verifying real platform behavior. Use before writing or editing code.
- file-placement: Where a file should live — the reference-distance metric, the high-reach code smell, and the mandated-location and test-location exemptions. Use before placing, moving, or renaming a file, or when reviewing where one lives.
- generate-project-instructions: Decompose a project into its pack facets (working-style class, technology, aspect, domain) and extract its working instructions into reusable canon packs plus the project's own local packs (.claudinite/local_packs/). Use when a project has no established working style yet, or it exhibits a facet no pack covers — during bootstrap of a fresh/empty repo, or on an uplevel request.
- prose-to-checks: Mine the corpus's existing prose (pack RULES.md, skill SKILL.md) for always-testable rules that were never converted to checks, and convert the strongest ones. Use when auditing the corpus for convertible rules, or when the nightly growth routine runs its prose-to-checks sweep.
- repo-text-sweeps: Mechanics for grep/sed sweeps, renames, and path relocations across a repo. Use before a bulk find-replace, a rename, or moving files — and after one, to catch silently broken references.
- unattended-agents: Architecture and practices for unattended, automation-invoked agents and recurring routines. Use when building, structuring, or running an AI agent, a scheduled routine, or a multi-stage agent pipeline.
- writing-claudinite-skills: Authoring or changing a skill in the Claudinite corpus — SKILL.md conventions, where a skill's checks and tests live, catalog and pack wiring. Use when creating or editing a pack's skills/<name>/ in the canon; a consumer project's own agent docs are authoring-agent-docs' turf.
- writing-tests: Practices for writing tests you can trust. Use before writing or changing any test — see-it-fail discipline, snapshot/golden rules, CI-only and heavy-browser tests, fuzzy-metric gating.
- add-live-case: Record a cached event page and author its reviewed live case for the extractor suite. Use when adding or refreshing a case under dev/requirements/extractor/ — new site support, a new behavior on a supported site, or re-recording a taken-down event page.
- git-github-advanced: Git/GitHub procedures beyond the baseline lifecycle. Use for commit layering, recovering a branch after a squash-merge, CI-trigger rules, GitHub Actions gotchas, or merge-relocation traps.
- lessons-learned: Retrospective over the current working session, converting its friction into durable improvements. Use when the owner says "learned lessons" / "lessons learned", and after every merge to main (the merge-to-main skill invokes it).
- merge-and-ci: Drive this repo's PR-to-merged flow cheaply — when to open the PR, how to get CI green in a Claude web session, and how to poll without wasting wall time or tokens. Use when merging a change, waiting on CI, or opening a PR that touches e2e/heavy/UI tests.
- merge-to-main: Merge the change in front of the owner into main. Use when the owner says "LGTM" or asks to merge/land the current branch or PR into main.
- single-branch-status: Assess one branch's landed status — merged, already-in-main, superseded, orphaned, or genuine unmerged work — read-only. Use when triaging whether a single branch is safe to delete, judging by content not the ref's name (e.g. the tidy-repo repo-tidy task hands you branches to assess).
- single-issue-triage: Triage one open issue and take the first applicable action — close-if-implemented, needs-decision, blocked, quick-win, or leave. Use when acting on a single issue; "implemented in main" must be verified against main's current content and the default is to comment, not close (e.g. the tidy-repo repo-tidy task hands you issues to triage).
- single-pr-status: Assess one open pull request's landed status — keep-open, or closeable (merged, already-in-main, superseded, stale) — read-only. Use when triaging whether a single PR should stay open, judging by its commits and diff not its title (e.g. the tidy-repo repo-tidy task hands you PRs to assess).
- snapshot-approval: Get owner approval for a moved UI-snapshot baseline. Use whenever a change to a dev/requirements case (its spec or its rendering) makes the snapshot tests fail — the pixels moved and the baseline wants to change.
- testing-guide: Map of this repo's test suites, the requirements model, and where each harness documents itself. Use before writing or changing tests here, adding a requirement case, or debugging a suite — the invariants live in the gcec pack's RULES.md; this is the deeper how-to.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact page can be granted — behavior static HTML cannot provide on its own, such as the page reading live or connected data, keeping state shared across viewers, or updating and republishing itself. Serves this user's live capability roster and the typed call definitions. Load it whenever the user asks for an artifact needing any such runtime behavior.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- morning: Render the user's morning brief as a styled HTML artifact, or set it up as a recurring weekday task. Use only when the user explicitly asks to run, see, or set up their morning brief, or if they invoke /morning by name. A question about their day, schedule, or calendar is not by itself a request for the brief; answer it directly instead.
- skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .xltx, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx or .potx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx or .potx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates (.potx), layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx or .potx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx or .potx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files) or Word templates (.dotx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', '.dotx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx or .dotx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

addedBlocks:
## github
The GitHub MCP Server provides tools to interact with GitHub platform.

Tool selection guidance:
	1. Use 'list_*' tools for broad, simple retrieval and pagination of all items of a type (e.g., all issues, all PRs, all branches) with basic filtering.
	2. Use 'search_*' tools for targeted queries with specific criteria, keywords, or complex filters (e.g., issues with certain text, PRs by author, code containing functions).

Context management:
	1. Use pagination whenever possible with batches of 5-10 items.
	2. Use minimal_output parameter set to true if the full information is not needed to accomplish a task.

Tool usage guidance:
	1. For 'search_*' tools: Use separate 'sort' and 'order' parameters if available for sorting results - do not include 'sort:' syntax in query strings. Query strings should contain only search criteria (e.g., 'org:google language:python'), not sorting instructions. Always call 'get_me' first to understand current user permissions and context. ## Issues

Check 'list_issue_types' first for organizations to use proper issue types. Use 'search_issues' before creating new issues to avoid duplicates. Always set 'state_reason' when closing issues. ## Pull Requests

PR review workflow: Always use 'pull_request_review_write' with method 'create' to create a pending review, then 'add_comment_to_pending_review' to add comments, and finally 'pull_request_review_write' with method 'submit_pending' to submit the review for complex reviews with line-specific comments.

Before creating a pull request, search for pull request templates in the repository. Template files are called pull_request_template.md or they're located in '.github/PULL_REQUEST_TEMPLATE' directory. Use the template content to structure the PR description and then call create_pull_request tool.

{"number":398,"title":"docs: record Phase 0 as-built deviations and carry-forward gaps","body":"Follow-up to the merged Phase 0 (#396): capture what landed **differently from the written plan** and what's **deliberately carried forward**, so a Phase 1 session can pick up the GCEC pilot without re-deriving it all.

## `MIGRATION.md` — a "Phase 0 landed" status block

**Deviations (as-built):**
- **No `scheduled-tasks` pack** — the two checks (`scheduler-workflow-shape`, `task-declaration-shape`) live in **basics** (doctrine in `packs/basics/scheduled-tasks.md`); scheduling is baseline discipline, active everywhere basics is.
- **Task conversions are additive** — `tasks/` beside an untouched `run_daily/`; `pack.mjs` unchanged (scheduler discovers `tasks/` from disk). `run_daily/` removed at Phase 4; the `run_daily`/`tasks` alias needed no code.
- **`in-session-github-access` needed no change** (already excludes `engine/`).
- **Two 0.4 rescopes deferred to Phase 1 cutover** (`no-scheduled-fleet-executor` rescope + `chrome-extension-release` cron flip) — they travel with the de-cron'd stub, per the migration's own ordering rule.

**Carry-forward gaps for Phase 1:**
- **The repo-hashed :10–:50 cron minute is not computed anywhere** — the stub ships a placeholder; GCEC cutover must build the hasher or the anti-stampede design fails.
- `store-release`'s inline worker is trigger-only (no await yet).
- The `fleet` signal collector is a stub (Phase 2 wiring).
- `run.mjs` + collectors are unit-tested only, never run against live GitHub.

## `DESIGN.md` §1

Notes that `tasks/` lands additively (legacy `run_daily/` removed at Phase 4) and the guards live in basics, pointing at the doctrine doc.

Docs-only; conformance clean.

Refs #394

content.text:
{"number":394,"title":"Implement per-project maintenance scheduling (owner-orchestrated rollout)","body":"Implementation tracker for the per-project scheduling conversion. Design + plan merged in #391 (Refs #390).

**Read first (on `main`):**
- Design — [`docs/per-project-scheduling/DESIGN.md`](https://github.com/missingbulb/Claudinite/blob/main/docs/per-project-scheduling/DESIGN.md)
- Migration plan — [`docs/per-project-scheduling/MIGRATION.md`](https://github.com/missingbulb/Claudinite/blob/main/docs/per-project-scheduling/MIGRATION.md)

**End state (DESIGN §8):** every repo schedules itself via a vendored hourly scheduler Action (the repo's only cron; hashed minute :10–:50) that evaluates per-task code preconditions and dispatches agent work as `[claudinite-task]` issues labeled `ready-for-agent`; a per-repo executor CCR routine fired by that label event runs each task at its declared model. `run_daily/` → `tasks//{task.mjs,task.md}`; `.claudinite/local_packs/` → `.claudinite/local/packs/` (declaration token `local/`). Fleet-scoped work (promote, discover-packs, migrations-retire, prose-to-checks, census) becomes ordinary tasks of the canon/sheepdog repos; baselining+migrations-apply become a per-repo basics self-refresh.

**Orchestration rules:** the owner drives each phase in a session; this rollout does **not** go through the regular migration-notes mechanism; the old central routine + the two GCEC triggers stay live (and are the rollback) until Phase 4; the `schedule` key in `.claudinite-checks.json` is the per-repo cutover marker, so exactly one mechanism owns a repo at any time.

**Phases (detail in MIGRATION.md):**
- [ ] **Phase 0 — canon groundwork:** build `engine/scheduler/`; add `schedule` to `CONFIG_KEYS`; `local_packs`→`local/packs` (dual-root scan) + rename the canon's own; convert canon pack tasks (baselining → per-repo self-refresh, store-release → absorbs the release cron); new/rescoped checks; doc rewrites; the `schedule`-key transition guard. No behavior change until a repo declares `schedule`.
- [ ] **Phase 1 — GCEC pilot:** vendor-refresh, scheduler workflow, strip the release cron, labels, move both `dev/routines/` into the gcec pack as `tasks/`, port the create-extractor triage *decision* into the precondition (fetch/scaffold stay in the task), write the `schedule` key, create the label-wired executor routine, disable the two old GCEC triggers. Soak ~1 week.
- [ ] **Phase 2 — canon cutover:** canon-side tasks (promote `daily`, discover-packs `weekly`, migrations-retire `daily+1h`, prose-to-checks `daily`), `fleet` signal over `FLEET_GITHUB_TOKEN`, sheepdog `fleet-census` task; canon repo gets its own scheduler + executor.
- [ ] **Phase 3 — remaining 9 consumers** (EdFringeNow, gRatio, TLDR, HelloWorldFlutterApp, EdFringeAllocator, CrosswordChat, Sheepdog, LaughCounter, ShoutsAndWhispers), batched ~3 at a time.
- [ ] **Phase 4 — decommission:** delete the central CCR trigger + both disabled GCEC triggers; remove `routines/auto-all-repos-maintenance.md` + `routines/fleet/`; drop the transition guard, `run_daily`/`local_packs` aliases; retire enrollment; final boxed review vs DESIGN §8.

**Independent fix (ship anytime, not gated on a phase):** `engine/pack_loader/inject-pack-prose.mjs` imports `registry.mjs` but the file is `pack-registry.mjs` — active packs' RULES.md are silently not injected at SessionStart, fleet-wide. One-line fix + regression test + vendor refresh.

* Fluent work(ctx) helper: shorter check-the-work rules, cached context reads

Add engine/checks/helpers/work.mjs — a null-safe fluent surface over ctx
(conversation().ownerTurns().last(), jsonPair, deadLinks,
danglingReferences, addedLines, branchCommits, introducedMerges,
filesContaining) — and rewrite the check-the-work rules against it,
trimming preamble comments to the load-bearing whys. Cache ctx.read and
ctx.readBase for the sweep: checks are read-only over an immutable
snapshot, and each readBase was a git subprocess per call.

Refs #392

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Claude-Session: https://claude.ai/code/session_015EGXi3y8hZNfS7A9csREv1

* Work rules take the work object: scope: 'work' on the rule, runRule dispatch

A rule declaring scope: 'work' now receives the fluent work view as its
run argument — the signature itself shows the rule judges the change, not
the repo. runRule (engine/checks/helpers/work.mjs) is the single dispatch
seam, used by the runner and every rule test; world rules keep the raw
ctx until a fluent world object lands (future change). The raw surface a
work rule still needs (files, read, exists, branch, commits, packConfig)
is delegated on Work, so work rules never touch ctx.

Refs #392

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Claude-Session: https://claude.ai/code/session_015EGXi3y8hZNfS7A9csREv1

* Split the runner by scope: check_the_world runs world rules, check_the_work runs work rules

The single runner ran every rule regardless of scope, so 'check the
world' meant 'run everything' and the work-scoped rules had no home of
their own. Extract the sweep pipeline into engine/checks/sweep.mjs and
give each scope its own thin entry: check_the_world.mjs runs the
repo-state rules plus the settings/interview diagnostics (themselves repo
state); check_the_work.mjs runs the rules judging the current change,
each dispatched with the fluent work view. A rule's scope ('work' or the
world default) decides which sweep runs it, in one place.

The Stop gate moves into engine/hooks/stop-command.mjs, which now
fast-exits then runs BOTH runners (work with the transcript) so the
session still sees every finding; CI runs both too. Also rename the work
rules' run argument w -> work and the transcript locals to
lastOwnerTurn / lastFeatureTurn, and re-home the barriers interview
exception onto sweep.mjs where the coupling now lives.

Refs #392

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Claude-Session: https://claude.ai/code/session_015EGXi3y8hZNfS7A9csREv1

* World sweep moves to the test/CI flow; Stop hook runs work scope only

The two scopes fire at different times because they answer different
questions. The work sweep judges the change in front of the session and
stays on the Stop hook (per-turn, with the transcript). The world sweep is
a whole-repo invariant assertion — the shape of a test suite — so it runs
wherever the project runs its tests, as its own standalone
check_the_world.mjs step (never a language-specific test file: a non-Node
consumer's runner can't load one, and the sweep is the engine's own
always-vendored Node CLI).

stop-command.mjs now runs only check_the_work.mjs — no more running both
runners every turn. Bootstrap wires the world step into the project's
test/CI flow, adding a minimal flow where the repo has none. This
supersedes the original #385 stance (Stop as the whole enforcement
surface, no consumer CI); the design record, README, bootstrap.md, and the
adopt-claudinite skill are updated to match, and the canon ci.yml models
the pattern.

Adds engine-tests/hooks/stop-command.test.mjs pinning that Stop surfaces
work findings and not world ones.

Refs #392

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Claude-Session: https://claude.ai/code/session_015EGXi3y8hZNfS7A9csREv1

* Make the two runners independent; delete the shared scope-branching sweep

sweep.mjs was a single runSweep(scope) both entries called, branching on
scope internally — re-coupling exactly what the split was meant to
separate. Worse, its top-level import of the adoption-interview machinery
(a grow_with_claudinite pack file) meant check_the_work transitively
depended on a pack it never touches, and the barriers core/content
exception landed on the shared file where it read as 'the pipeline
depends on a pack'.

check_the_world.mjs and check_the_work.mjs are now fully independent —
neither imports the other or any shared runner. They share only two
scope-blind mechanism helpers with descriptive names:
  - run-active-pack-rules.mjs — run the active packs' rules a predicate
    admits (each runner passes its own scope predicate; no scope logic here)
  - report-findings.mjs — apply config, sort, print, return blocking count
The world runner alone carries the settings/interview diagnostics, the
adopt-claudinite interview import, and the barriers exception (moved back
to check_the_world.mjs with a rewritten, legible reason). The work runner
now has zero pack dependency — the barrier check actively guards this
(a pack import into it is flagged BLOCKING).

CI runs the world sweep only (owner decision): the work scope judges a
session's change and runs at that session's Stop hook, not in CI.
bootstrap.md, ci.yml, and engine/checks/README.md updated to match.

Refs #392

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Claude-Session: https://claude.ai/code/session_015EGXi3y8hZNfS7A9csREv1

* check_the_world names no pack: interview hygiene becomes a skill-owned check

The world runner had a special mechanism baked in — it imported the
adoption-interview machinery from a specific pack to emit stale-answer and
malformed-questions diagnostics. That single pack import was the entire
reason for the barriers core/content exception on check_the_world.mjs.

Now the runner just runs every active pack's world rules plus pack-agnostic
config/load integrity (malformed JSON, unknown pack, broken pack.mjs) — and
imports no pack:
  - The generic `questions` manifest-shape validator (packQuestions) moves
    into the pack loader, so a malformed `questions` field is an ordinary
    load fault in packErrors, reported pack-agnostically.
  - The interview HYGIENE (a stored answer whose question its pack no longer
    declares) becomes a skill-owned world check in the lifecycle pack's
    adopt-claudinite skill, reading the discovered packs the runner now
    attaches to ctx (ctx.packs). It rides that pack's activation like any
    skill check, so a repo without the lifecycle pack never runs it.
  - --init no longer prints pending questions (the adopt skill and the
    SessionStart interview-check step drive the interview); the runner drops
    its interviewState/loadConfig imports.

The barriers exception for check_the_world.mjs is deleted — the runner names
no pack, and the barrier check actively enforces that (a pack mention, even
in a comment, is flagged BLOCKING). SessionStart's interview-check step is
untouched — it invokes interview.mjs as a subprocess by path, not an import.

Refs #392

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Claude-Session: https://claude.ai/code/session_015EGXi3y8hZNfS7A9csREv1

Refs #193","author":{"name":"missingbulb","email":"73882448+missingbulb@users.noreply.github.com","date":"2026-07-22T13:52:18Z"},"committer":{"name":"GitHub","email":"noreply@github.com","date":"2026-07-22T13:52:18Z"}},"author":{"login":"missingbulb","id":73882448,"profile_url":"https://github.com/missingbulb","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/73882448?v=4"},"committer":{"login":"web-flow","id":19864447,"profile_url":"https://github.com/web-flow","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/19864447?v=4"}},{"sha":"47cdbf484de9ad80ed58966151d0d711ba3c2b1d","html_url":"https://github.com/missingbulb/Claudinite/commit/47cdbf484de9ad80ed58966151d0d711ba3c2b1d","commit":{"message":"Fix silent pack-prose injection failure: correct registry import path (#395)

inject-pack-prose.mjs (the SessionStart load-active-prose step) imported
`registry.mjs`, but the module is `pack-registry.mjs`. The dynamic import
rejected, the fail-soft catch swallowed it, and every active pack's RULES.md
was silently never injected into session context — fleet-wide, since the
engine vendors verbatim into every consumer. The sibling steps
(mount-skills.mjs, env-requirements.mjs) already import the correct name.

- One-line fix: `registry.mjs` -> `pack-registry.mjs`.
- Regression test: engine-tests/pack_loader/inject-pack-prose.test.mjs runs
  the real script against a hermetic corpus and the real corpus, asserting
  active packs' prose IS emitted (not just exit 0). Fails on the bug, passes
  on the fix.
- Vendor refresh: none needed in-canon — the engine is vendored wholesale, so
  the nightly baselining pass re-vendors the corrected file to every member
  from canon HEAD once this lands.
- Drop the now-completed to-do from the per-project-scheduling migration doc.

Refs #397

inject-pack-prose.mjs (the SessionStart load-active-prose step) imported
`registry.mjs`, but the module is `pack-registry.mjs`. The dynamic import
rejected, the fail-soft catch swallowed it, and every active pack's RULES.md
was silently never injected into session context — fleet-wide, since the
engine vendors verbatim into every consumer. The sibling steps
(mount-skills.mjs, env-requirements.mjs) already import the correct name.

- One-line fix: `registry.mjs` -> `pack-registry.mjs`.
- Regression test: engine-tests/pack_loader/inject-pack-prose.test.mjs runs
  the real script against a hermetic corpus and the real corpus, asserting
  active packs' prose IS emitted (not just exit 0). Fails on the bug, passes
  on the fix.
- Vendor refresh: none needed in-canon — the engine is vendored wholesale, so
  the nightly baselining pass re-vendors the corrected file to every member
  from canon HEAD once this lands.
- Drop the now-completed to-do from the per-project-scheduling migration doc.

1 # Per-project scheduling — project & migration plan 2 3 Refs #390. Executes [DESIGN.md](DESIGN.md). The owner orchestrates every phase in 4 sessions; this rollout deliberately does **not** use the regular maintenance 5 mechanism (no `migrations/active_migrations/` note, no nightly-driven 6 conversion) — the old machinery keeps running untouched until Phase 4 and is the 7 rollback at every step. 8 9 Ground rules for every phase: 10 11 - Pilot before fleet: nothing reaches a second repo until GCEC has soaked (the 12 standing "pilot on one real consumer" rule). 13 - Never break the channel the migration travels through: the central routine and 14 the vendor-refresh path stay functional until Phase 4 decommission. 15 - Cutover per repo is marked by the `schedule` key in `.claudinite-checks.json`: 16 the central planner skips any member that declares it (guard added in 17 Phase 0.6), so a repo is covered by exactly one mechanism at all times — 18 no double runs during the staggered rollout. 19 - Each phase ends with its verification step green before the next starts. 20 21 --- 22 23 ## Phase 0 — canon groundwork (Claudinite PRs; no behavior change anywhere) 24 25 1. **Build `engine/scheduler/`** (vendored to consumers with the engine): 26 `run.mjs` (entry), `slots.mjs` (due-slot math over the workflow-run ledger), 27 `discover.mjs` (uniform `.claudinite/{shared,local}/packs/*/tasks/*` scan, 28 activation-gated), `signals/.mjs` collectors (`commits`, `prs`, 29 `issues`, `branches`, `release`, `localPacks`, `sharedMount`, 30 `conversationLogs`, `stamp`, `fleet`), `dispatch.mjs` (issue 31 search/create/label + at-most-one-open + stale escalation), 32 `validate-dispatch.mjs`, `verify-outcome.mjs`, `model-map.mjs`, 33 `executor.md` (including the drain-other-ready-issues sweep), and the 34 workflow stub `stubs/claudinite-scheduler.yml` (hashed cron minute in 35 **:10–:50**, `concurrency`, thin shim calling the vendored engine). 36 2. **Engine schema & layout**: add `schedule` to `CONFIG_KEYS` with load-time 37 validation (`dailyHour` 0–23, `weeklyDay` Sun–Sat, `monthlyDay` 1–31) and the 38 documented defaults. Implement the `local_packs` → `local/packs` rename 39 engine-side: `LOCAL_PACKS_SUBDIR` moves to `.claudinite/local/packs`, 40 discovery scans **both roots** until Phase 4, canonical declaration token 41 becomes `local/` (legacy `local_packs/` and bare ids accepted; 42 baselining's normalization rewrites them). Rename the canon's own 43 `.claudinite/local_packs/` in the same change. 44 3. **Convert canon pack tasks**: each `packs/

/run_daily/.mjs` + 45 `.worker.md` → `packs/

/tasks//{task.mjs,task.md}` with the 46 declarations per DESIGN.md §6 (grow_with_claudinite's root-level worker docs 47 move into their task dirs). Gates become preconditions over the new signals 48 object; `smarts` → `model`; `full_sweep_supported` dropped. Two conversions 49 are structural, not mechanical: 50 - **baselining** becomes the per-repo self-refresh task in basics 51 (`daily-2h`): converge own mount from the in-session canon checkout, apply 52 pending migration notes (the fleet apply pass folds in), advance the stamp; 53 precondition on `stamp`/`sharedMount` with stamp-age fallback; skips 54 naturally where no shared mount exists (the canon). 55 - **store-release** gains its Stage 2: the inline worker dispatches the 56 release workflow in daily mode and awaits it; the stub loses its 57 `schedule:` cron (ships to consumers in their cutover, together with the 58 check flip below). 59 4. **Checks**: new `task-declaration-shape` (every `tasks/*/task.mjs` exports the 60 full contract with legal enum values) and `scheduler-workflow-shape` (cron 61 minute :10–:50, concurrency, dispatch shape); rescope 62 `gha/no-scheduled-fleet-executor` to "the vendored scheduler workflow is the 63 repo's only permitted cron"; flip 64 `chrome-extension-release/release-workflows.mjs` from requiring the contract 65 cron to **forbidding any cron** (lands in a consumer's mount together with 66 the de-cron'd stub — never before it); scope `in-session-github-access` to 67 exclude `engine/scheduler/` Action-side code; extend the routine-structure 68 check to the task-folder convention. 69 5. **Docs**: rewrite `routines/fleet/scheduling.md` (new doctrine), 70 `bootstrap.md` Part 6 (per DESIGN.md §9), unattended-agents SKILL.md 71 (task-folder shape + issue-dispatch security rule), `packs/README.md`, 72 `extending.md` (`run_daily` → `tasks`, `local_packs` → `local/packs`), 73 `routines/fleet/DESIGN.md` header pointing here, and the growth-stage docs' 74 capture-surface definition + claudinite-isolation carve-outs for the new 75 local-pack path. (The sheepdog census classification note landed with the 76 design PR.) 77 6. **Transition guard** (small, ships first): the central planner skips members 78 whose `.claudinite-checks.json` declares `schedule`, and treats `tasks/` / 79 `run_daily/` and both local-pack roots as aliases until Phase 4. 80 7. **Verify**: canon CI green; engine tests cover slot math (miss → catch-up, 81 double-run → dedupe, first-run, month-clamp, hour-wrap), dispatch 82 exactly-once + at-most-one-open, validate-dispatch accept/reject fixtures, 83 dual-root discovery; one manual `workflow_dispatch` of the scheduler on the 84 canon repo in dry-run mode prints a sane job summary. 85 86 ## Phase 1 — pilot on GCEC 87 88 1. In-session vendor refresh of GCEC (brings the scheduler engine + converted 89 canon packs + flipped release check); copy `claudinite-scheduler.yml` into 90 `.github/workflows/`; **strip the `schedule:` cron from the release 91 workflow** in the same commit; create labels `ready-for-agent` / 92 `agent-running` / `needs-human` (`workflow-failure` exists). 93 2. `git mv .claudinite/local_packs .claudinite/local/packs`; rewrite the 94 declaration token to `local/gcec`; sweep references (text-sweep skill). 95 3. Move `dev/routines/create-extractor/` → 96 `.claudinite/local/packs/gcec/tasks/create-extractor/` and 97 `dev/routines/auto-fallback-coverage/` → 98 `.claudinite/local/packs/gcec/tasks/auto-fallback-coverage/` 99 (`routine.md` → `task.md`, scripts beside). Write the two `task.mjs` 100 declarations (DESIGN.md §6). Port `2-triage.js`'s deterministic *decision* 101 into the create-extractor precondition — deny/allow/duplicate closes with the 102 canned message in-scheduler; **fetching and scaffolding stay in the task**. 103 Update every reference: gcec `RULES.md`, `CLAUDE.md`, 104 `dev/procedures/fileDescriptions.md`, the extension popup comment. Fold in 105 the drift fixes: gcec README's missing add-live-case row; CLAUDE.md's stale 106 "checks run in CI" claim. 107 4. Write `"schedule": { "dailyHour": 4, "weeklyDay": "Sun", "monthlyDay": 1 }` 108 into GCEC's `.claudinite-checks.json` — the cutover marker: the central 109 routine stops planning GCEC the same night (self-baselining takes over the 110 refresh). 111 5. Create the GCEC executor routine — **label-wired**: fires on the 112 `ready-for-agent` label event, `sonnet`, launcher prompt 113 `Execute the Claudinite executor: .claudinite/shared/engine/scheduler/executor.md`, 114 sources = GCEC + Claudinite (as the old triggers had). **Disable — do not 115 delete** — the two old GCEC CCR triggers. 116 6. **Soak ≈1 week, verify**: hourly summaries list evaluations with sane 117 skip-reasons; one real `extractor-request` flows end-to-end (precondition 118 context → dispatch issue → label event → executor → review PR, in minutes); 119 one auto-fallback-coverage daily run fires on a meaningful-commit day and 120 skips a quiet day; one self-baselining run converges the mount and advances 121 the stamp; the release task dispatches (or correctly skips) the de-cron'd 122 release workflow; never more than one open dispatch issue per task; the 123 scheduler does not self-trigger on its own issues/commits; a deliberately 124 mangled dispatch issue (bad first line) is rejected by `validate-dispatch` 125 and converged to `needs-human`. 126 7. Rollback: disable the scheduler workflow, remove the `schedule` key, restore 127 the release cron, re-enable the old triggers. 128 129 ## Phase 2 — canon repo cutover 130 131 1. Add the canon-side tasks (DESIGN.md §6 table 2) to 132 `canon-curation/tasks/`: `growth-promote` (daily, `fleet`), 133 `growth-discover-packs` (weekly, `fleet` — moves from member-scheduled to one 134 central weekly sweep), `migrations-retire` (daily+1h, `none`, `fleet` — 135 stamp + `legacyPresent` probe evidence; no artifact plumbing), and 136 `prose-to-checks-sweep` (**daily**, canon-local, no fleet signal). The 137 sheepdog repo gains `tasks/fleet-census/` (daily, `none`) with the 138 classification note in the task file. 139 2. Build the `fleet` signal collector over the fleet PAT (`FLEET_GITHUB_TOKEN` 140 Actions secret — the census's existing credential). 141 3. Canon repo: `schedule` key (dailyHour 4), scheduler workflow, label-wired 142 executor routine, labels — same as any member. The central routine keeps 143 running for **unmigrated consumers only** (the Phase 0.6 guard also skips the 144 canon's own pack tasks, which the canon scheduler now owns). 145 4. **Verify**: one full night where baselining (02:00) → extract (pilot repos, 146 03:00) → promote (canon, 04:00, picking up the night's merged extracts) → 147 dedup (05:00) ran in order via the new path; migrations-retire evaluates the 148 guard from stamps + probes 149 and correctly retires nothing (or stages a real retirement PR); promote's 150 `fleet` signal lists exactly the members whose local packs changed; 151 prose-to-checks fires daily and no-ops cheaply on a quiet corpus. 152 153 ## Phase 3 — remaining consumers (owner-orchestrated, batched ~3 at a time) 154 155 Repos: EdFringeNow, gRatio, TLDR, HelloWorldFlutterApp, EdFringeAllocator, 156 CrosswordChat, Sheepdog, LaughCounter, ShoutsAndWhispers. 157 158 Per-repo checklist (self-contained; one session each): 159 160 1. In-session vendor refresh; copy the scheduler workflow; create the labels; 161 strip any release cron (chrome-extension repos). 162 2. `git mv .claudinite/local_packs .claudinite/local/packs` (where present) + 163 token rewrite + reference sweep; convert any local `run_daily/` → `tasks/` 164 (most repos: none). 165 3. Write the `schedule` key (cutover marker). 166 4. Create the label-wired executor routine (sonnet, thin-pointer prompt, 167 sources = repo + Claudinite). 168 5. Verify: first scheduler summary sane; central routine's next night skips the 169 repo; a hand-labeled test dispatch issue executes and closes. 170 171 ## Phase 4 — decommission & cleanup 172 173 1. Delete the "All Missing Bulb Repos - Daily Maintenance" CCR trigger; delete 174 the two disabled GCEC triggers. 175 2. Remove `routines/auto-all-repos-maintenance.md` and `routines/fleet/` 176 (planner, registry, local-tasks, schedule, gates, signals + tests); drop the 177 Phase 0.6 transition guard, the `run_daily`/`tasks` alias, and the 178 `local_packs` dual-root scan. 179 3. Retire enrollment: close any open `Enroll … in Claudinite fleet maintenance` 180 issues; remove baselining's enrollment-close step and Part 6 remnants. 181 4. Repo-wide sweep (text-sweep skill): `run_daily`, `local_packs`, `fullSweep`, 182 `full_sweep_supported`, `smarts`, `auto-all-repos-maintenance` — every 183 surviving reference is either historical (this folder) or updated. 184 5. **Final review with the owner** (boxed): the end-state schedule inventory — 185 per-repo scheduler workflows (the only crons) + label-fired executor 186 routines + the canon task table — matches DESIGN.md §8 exactly. 187 188 ## Independent fixes (not gated on any phase) 189 190 - The auto-fallback-coverage weekly-cron/daily-spec mismatch is *fixed by* 191 Phase 1; until then it stands — no interim change. 192 193 ## Risks 194 195 | Risk | Mitigation | 196 |---|---| 197 | GitHub cron unreliability / :00 stampede | Repo-hashed minute in :10–:50; run-ledger catch-up makes any miss self-healing. | 198 | Trigger API unavailable at bootstrap/migration time | Enclosed-config owner issue fallback (DESIGN.md §9). | 199 | Missed / duplicate label events | Executor's drain-other-ready-issues sweep + scheduler stale-issue escalation; `ready-for-agent` → `agent-running` claim swap. | 200 | Issue overload | At-most-one open dispatch issue per task, by construction; hourly tasks never backfill. | 201 | Fleet reads of private members from Actions | The existing `FLEET_GITHUB_TOKEN` PAT, only on the canon/sheepdog repos; consumers never need it. | 202 | Model family drift | Single vendored `model-map.mjs`. | 203 | Check/stub ordering (release cron flip) | The forbidding check and the de-cron'd stub travel in the same per-repo cutover commit. | 204 | Stranded repos mid-rollout | The `schedule`-key guard gives every repo exactly one owner at all times; central machinery deleted only in Phase 4. | 205 206 Out of scope: Yestersummary (untouched). 207

The sheepdog repo gains `tasks/fleet-census/` (daily, `none`) with the classification note in the task file. 2. Build the `fleet` signal collector over the fleet PAT (`FLEET_GITHUB_TOKEN` Actions secret — the census's existing credential). 3. Canon repo: `schedule` key (dailyHour 4), scheduler workflow, label-wired executor routine, labels — same as any member. The central routine keeps running for **unmigrated consumers only** (the Phase 0.6 guard also skips the canon's own pack tasks, which the canon scheduler now owns). 4. **Verify**: one full night where baselining (02:00) → extract (pilot repos, 03:00) → promote (canon, 04:00, picking up the night's merged extracts) → dedup (05:00) ran in order via the new path; migrations-retire evaluates the guard from stamps + probes and correctly retires nothing (or stages a real retirement PR); promote's `fleet` signal lists exactly the members whose local packs changed; prose-to-checks fires daily and no-ops cheaply on a quiet corpus. ## Phase 3 — remaining consumers (owner-orchestrated, batched ~3 at a time) Repos: EdFringeNow, gRatio, TLDR, HelloWorldFlutterApp, EdFringeAllocator, CrosswordChat, Sheepdog, LaughCounter, ShoutsAndWhispers. Per-repo checklist (self-contained; one session each): 1. In-session vendor refresh; copy the scheduler workflow; create the labels; strip any release cron (chrome-extension repos). 2. `git mv .claudinite/local_packs .claudinite/local/packs` (where present) + token rewrite + reference sweep; convert any local `run_daily/` → `tasks/` (most repos: none). 3. Write the `schedule` key (cutover marker). 4. Create the label-wired executor routine (sonnet, thin-pointer prompt, sources = repo + Claudinite). 5. Verify: first scheduler summary sane; central routine's next night skips the repo; a hand-labeled test dispatch issue executes and closes. ## Phase 4 — decommission & cleanup 1. Delete the "All Missing Bulb Repos - Daily Maintenance" CCR trigger; delete the two disabled GCEC triggers. 2. Remove `routines/auto-all-repos-maintenance.md` and `routines/fleet/` (planner, registry, local-tasks, schedule, gates, signals + tests); drop the Phase 0.6 transition guard, the `run_daily`/`tasks` alias, and the `local_packs` dual-root scan. 3. Retire enrollment: close any open `Enroll … in Claudinite fleet maintenance` issues; remove baselining's enrollment-close step and Part 6 remnants. 4. Repo-wide sweep (text-sweep skill): `run_daily`, `local_packs`, `fullSweep`, `full_sweep_supported`, `smarts`, `auto-all-repos-maintenance` — every surviving reference is either historical (this folder) or updated. 5. **Final review with the owner** (boxed): the end-state schedule inventory — per-repo scheduler workflows (the only crons) + label-fired executor routines + the canon task table — matches DESIGN.md §8 exactly. ## Independent fixes (not gated on any phase) - The auto-fallback-coverage weekly-cron/daily-spec mismatch is *fixed by* Phase 1; until then it stands — no interim change. ## Risks | Risk | Mitigation | |---|---| | GitHub cron unreliability / :00 stampede | Repo-hashed minute in :10–:50; run-ledger catch-up makes any miss self-healing. | | Trigger API unavailable at bootstrap/migration time | Enclosed-config owner issue fallback (DESIGN.md §9). | | Missed / duplicate label events | Executor's drain-other-ready-issues sweep + scheduler stale-issue escalation; `ready-for-agent` → `agent-running` claim swap. | | Issue overload | At-most-one open dispatch issue per task, by construction; hourly tasks never backfill. | | Fleet reads of private members from Actions | The existing `FLEET_GITHUB_TOKEN` PAT, only on the canon/sheepdog repos; consumers never need it. | | Model family drift | Single vendored `model-map.mjs`. | | Check/stub ordering (release cron flip) | The forbidding check and the de-cron'd stub travel in the same per-repo cutover commit. | | Stranded repos mid-rollout | The `schedule`-key guard gives every repo exactly one owner at all times; central machinery deleted only in Phase 4. | Out of scope: Yestersummary (untouched).

content.text: Now I have the full Phase 1 plan.
